agent_raw: The AutoML insurance benchmark cannot be accepted by the steering committee. Two methodological problems invalidate the claimed advantage. First, preprocessing — including target encoding for high-cardinality fields and median imputation — was applied to the entire two-year modeling table before cross-validation folds were created. Target encoding computes the mean target value per category across the full dataset. When this encoding is used in cross-validation, each fold's 'held-out' evaluation data has already contributed its outcomes to the feature values used for training. This is preprocessing leakage. The CV AUC from this design is an optimistic estimate. Target encoding is a sound technique when computed within each training fold — the issue is purely about the order of operations. Second, the final operating threshold was selected to achieve 80% recall on the held-out test set. Even though 80% recall is an operational requirement, using the test set to identify which threshold achieves it makes the precision estimate at that threshold in-sample. An independent test set evaluation requires that the threshold be fixed before the test set is evaluated — threshold selection should be done on validation data. Additionally, the baseline logistic regression retains its original validation-set threshold from a different project protocol, making the comparison with the AutoML system — which has a test-set-optimized threshold — asymmetric. A corrected evaluation with fold-specific preprocessing and matched threshold-selection protocols is required before any implementation decision.
all_issues_raised: Target encoding fitted on full table before fold creation — CV AUC inflated
Operating threshold selected on test set — precision estimate contaminated
Baseline uses different threshold protocol than AutoML system
